title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: CISA flags an auth-identity leak in ST Engineering iDirect satellite terminals used across the merchant fleet
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-183-01 (2 July) covers ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series VSAT terminals — the satcom gear that keeps merchant vessels connected at sea. An unauthenticated attacker with network access to the terminal’s management API (CVE-2026-38059) can retrieve the Device ID (DID) and the Terminal Private Key identifier (TPK) the unit uses to authenticate to the satellite network, opening the door to terminal impersonation and network reconnaissance; a second cross-site request forgery flaw targets the reboot endpoint. Affected models are the Evolution iQ-Series, 3315-Series and 9-Series at versions 4.5.2.1 and earlier, with the fix in 4.5.2.2. For fleet and IT-OT managers the framing is uncomfortable: the box that keeps a ship online can be made to leak its own satellite-auth identity to anyone who can reach its management interface. No public exploitation is reported.

items.title: GNSS jamming and spoofing surge blinds vessel navigation across the Red Sea and Gulf
items.content_text: Maritime-security bulletins in mid-July flag elevated GNSS interference as an ongoing operational hazard, with Cydome’s 10 July bulletin framing the current period as one of sustained activity. Marlink recorded a more than 50% surge in GNSS interference across its customer fleet, concentrated in the Middle East and other high-tension corridors; affected ships have appeared on land, at impossible speeds or circling, and the MSC Antonia grounded in the Red Sea near Jeddah after spoofing placed it hundreds of miles off its true position. Spoofing can also degrade GMDSS distress signalling. This is electronic-warfare disruption of position, navigation and timing — not a confirmed cyber-OT compromise of vessel or terminal control systems — but the operational impact on the bridge is real and immediate.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Cydome bulletin / Marlink via Industrial Cyber / MarineInsight

items.title: Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the bridges between shipboard serial gear and the network
items.content_text: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, one of the flaws disclosed in April through the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. This category deserves particular attention at sea and on the quayside. Maritime OT is still overwhelmingly serial at the sensor and actuator layer — NMEA feeds, engine and auxiliary monitoring, alarm and monitoring panels, cargo and ballast systems, crane and gate equipment — and serial-to-IP converters are what connected all of it to shipboard or terminal Ethernet during retrofits and digitalisation projects. They are typically installed by an integrator, absent from any drawing, and never patched because the equipment behind them still works. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning; for most operators the first task is establishing what is actually installed.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

items.title: FSB Center 16 router-config harvesting (AA26-194A) — the same edge gear runs port and shipboard networks
items.content_text: On 13 July, NSA, CISA and the FBI, joined by roughly a dozen international partners, warned that Russia’s FSB Center 16 harvests configuration data from routers that expose SNMP and Cisco Smart Install, using the collected configs to map networks and enable follow-on intrusions. The advisory is written for enterprise networks, but the reframing for maritime is direct: port terminal-operating-system networks and shipboard routers run the same exposed edge gear, often installed by integrators and rarely audited. A harvested config hands an adversary the layout of the port or vessel network — addressing, VLANs, trust relationships — before they ever touch a control system. This is confirmed nation-state collection activity, not a single incident, but it targets exactly the infrastructure marine operators tend to overlook.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: NSA / CISA / FBI joint advisory AA26-194A (+ ~12 partners)

items.title: USCG Cyber Command’s fifth CTIME report: reported maritime cyber incidents up 17%, phishing dominant
items.content_text: Coast Guard Cyber Command (CGCYBER) released its fifth annual Cyber Trends and Insights in the Marine Environment (CTIME) report, drawing on a record 42 Cyber Protection Team missions plus industry telemetry from 2025. Reported maritime cyber incidents rose 17% year-over-year. Phishing was the initial-access vector in 43% of incidents — an 18-point jump — with syndicates such as Scattered Spider pairing phishing with voice impersonation (vishing) to defeat help desks and weak MFA; ransomware featured in 19% of attack paths, and OT was in scope for 62% of the command’s missions. CGCYBER stresses that the blurring IT/OT boundary aboard ships, machinery and port facilities raises the odds of a cyber event producing physical consequences. Notably, Cyber Protection Teams deployed alongside boarding teams in Dark Fleet interdiction operations for the first time.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Fifth annual CTIME report — released Jul 2026 (2025 data)

items.title: SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days under active attack; both added to CISA KEV
items.content_text: SonicWall confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) lets an authenticated administrator inject OS commands through the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal deadline. For ports, terminals and vessel operators the placement is what matters: this class of appliance brokers the shoreside and vendor access that reaches terminal operating systems, crane and gate control, and satcom-backhauled vessel networks. A confirmed compromise is also a reportable-incident question under the USCG rule, where notification to the National Response Center is due immediately on discovery — a notification decision, not just a patch.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — exploitation ongoing

items.title: July ICS Patch Tuesday: Siemens ships a CVSS 10 auth bypass fix; Rockwell patches critical controller DoS flaws
items.content_text: The July 2026 cycle matters to terminals and shipyards running mainstream industrial automation. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical — headlined by a CVSS 10 token-invalidation flaw in Opencenter X allowing authentication bypass and full application access, with further critical fixes in Simatic S7-1500, Mendix, Sidis Secured SmartPlug, Cadra and Desigo CC (the last being building-management software common in terminal and port facilities). Rockwell published 12 advisories including three critical denial-of-service holes in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault, plus a critical unauthenticated CLI exposure in 1715 Redundant I/O. Schneider fixed a high-severity code-execution flaw in IGSS SCADA. VDE CERT added five advisories covering Codesys and Wago among others.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / Rockwell PSIRT / SecurityWeek

items.title: The maritime cyber compliance clock: USCG MTS final rule, IMO cyber-risk expectations, and IACS UR E26/E27
items.content_text: A forward-looking set of obligations operators must be able to evidence now, not news. The USCG’s Cyber Risk Management for the Marine Transportation System final rule applies to U.S.-flagged vessels, OCS facilities and waterfront facilities on a phased schedule — reportable incidents to the National Response Center immediately on discovery since 16 July 2025, personnel cyber training since 16 January 2026, and a designated Cybersecurity Officer, completed assessment and USCG-approved Cybersecurity Plan due 16 July 2027. Running alongside it are IMO cyber-risk-management expectations for safety-management systems and the IACS Unified Requirements UR E26 (ship cyber resilience) and UR E27 (onboard systems and equipment), which apply to ships contracted for construction on or after 1 January 2024. Together they push cyber risk management from a shoreside IT concern into classed newbuild and vessel-operation requirements.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Dragos (USCG MTSA / IMO / IACS overview) / US Coast Guard
_otthreatfeed.date_note: USCG training due 16 Jan 2026; CySO/assessment/plan due 16 Jul 2027; IACS E26/E27 for ships contracted from 1 Jan 2024
